coredata.dc:title: A novel dual-mode colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence method for detecting isocarbophos based on S, N-CDs inhibiting Fe3O4@Cu-BTC nanozyme activity
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Lijun
Tang, Jiangchuan
Ren, Shanshan
Hu, Haixia
Yang, Xiao
Chen, Xianggui
coredata.dc:description: Although many colorimetric methods have been developed for on-site pesticide detection, most exhibit low sensitivity and limited resistance to interference. To address these issues, this study developed a novel dual-mode colorimetric-ratiometric fluorescence method for isocarbophos (ICP) detection using sulfur and nitrogen co-doped carbon nanodots (S, N-CDs) to inhibit catalytic Fe3O4@Cu-BTC nanozyme activity. The synthesized Fe3O4@Cu-BTC nanozymes with higher peroxidase-like activity were inhibited by S, N-CDs. The inhibitory mechanism was deeply investigated and was ascribed to (1) the restriction of the reaction between Fe3O4@Cu-BTC and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), which decreased the ·OH production, and (2) the ·OH scavenging ability of the S, N-CDs, forming the basis of the dual-mode method. The Fe3O4@Cu-BTC/Apt-complementary strand (CS)/S, N-CDs hybridization complexes demonstrated minimal to no peroxidase-like activity, preventing H2O2 from oxidizing o-phenylenediamine (OPD) to produce yellow fluorescent 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP). Meanwhile, the detection system demonstrated a low fluorescence intensity ratio (I575/I465) at an excitation wavelength of 390 nm. However, the presence of ICP caused the CS/S, N-CDs to separate from the Fe3O4@Cu-BTC/Apt due to the high affinity between Apt and ICP. Then, the Fe3O4@Cu-BTC/Apt-ICP complexes recovered peroxidase-like activity, which promoted DAP production, accompanied by a pronounced fluorescence intensity ratio (I575/I465). In optimum conditions, the detection limits (LODs) were 2.79 ng/mL (colorimetric) and 0.26 ng/mL (ratiometric fluorescence), respectively. The feasibility of the proposed method was verified via three spiked samples, yielding recovery rates of between 86.08 % and 112.64 %, indicating the significant potential for the highly sensitive, accurate on-site detection of ICP in actual samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Isocarbophos
Dual-mode method
Fe3O4@Cu-BTC nanozymes
Carbon nanodots
Colorimetric method
Ratiometric fluorescence method

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the CS/S, N-CDs probe
Determination of ICP in real samples
The mechanism of Fe3O4@Cu-BTC catalytic activity inhibition by S, N-CDs
Detection of ICP in actual samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM of Fe3O4 nanozymes (A) and Fe3O4@Cu-BTC nanozymes (B); Zeta potential (C), XRD (D), FTIR (E) and the peroxidase-like activity (F) of Fe3O4 nanozymes and Fe3O4@Cu-BTC nanozymes.
TEM (A) and FTIR (B) of S, N-CDs; Changes in emission wavelengths at different excitation wavelengths (C).
Fluorescence changes (A) and absorbance changes (B) of different systems; feasibility study on of ratiometric fluorescence detection (C) and colorimetric detection (D).

Absorption spectra of various solutions using TMB (A), OPD (B) and ABTS(C) as hydrogen donor substrate, respectively. The EPR spectra of Fe3O4@Cu-BTC nanozymes(D); Free radical scavenging test (E); Fluorescent spectra of the system containing Fe3O4@Cu-BTC nanozymes(F); Fluorescent spectra of the system without Fe3O4@Cu-BTC nanozymes(G).

The absorbance spectra at different ICP concentrations (A); the linear correlation of the absorbance value with ICP concentration (B); the linear relationship of RGB with ICP concentration (C); the fluorescence spectra at different ICP concentrations (D); and the linear relationship between I575/I465 with ICP concentration (E).
The principle of dual-mode method for detecting ICP.
A comparison of the kinetic parameters for different nanozymes.

head.ce:title: A novel dual-mode colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence method for detecting isocarbophos based on S, N-CDs inhibiting Fe3O4@Cu-BTC nanozyme activity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Food and Bioengineering, Xihua University, Chengdu, 610039, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Food and Bioengineering, Xihua University, Chengdu 610039, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Food Microbiology Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province, Xihua University, Chengdu, 610039, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Food Microbiology Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Food Microbiology Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province, Xihua University, Chengdu 610039, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Food and Bioengineering, Xihua University, Chengdu, 610039, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Although many colorimetric methods have been developed for on-site pesticide detection, most exhibit low sensitivity and limited resistance to interference. To address these issues, this study developed a novel dual-mode colorimetric-ratiometric fluorescence method for isocarbophos (ICP) detection using sulfur and nitrogen co-doped carbon nanodots (S, N-CDs) to inhibit catalytic Fe3O4@Cu-BTC nanozyme activity. The synthesized Fe3O4@Cu-BTC nanozymes with higher peroxidase-like activity were inhibited by S, N-CDs. The inhibitory mechanism was deeply investigated and was ascribed to (1) the restriction of the reaction between Fe3O4@Cu-BTC and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), which decreased the ·OH production, and (2) the ·OH scavenging ability of the S, N-CDs, forming the basis of the dual-mode method. The Fe3O4@Cu-BTC/Apt-complementary strand (CS)/S, N-CDs hybridization complexes demonstrated minimal to no peroxidase-like activity, preventing H2O2 from oxidizing o-phenylenediamine (OPD) to produce yellow fluorescent 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP). Meanwhile, the detection system demonstrated a low fluorescence intensity ratio (I575/I465) at an excitation wavelength of 390 nm. However, the presence of ICP caused the CS/S, N-CDs to separate from the Fe3O4@Cu-BTC/Apt due to the high affinity between Apt and ICP. Then, the Fe3O4@Cu-BTC/Apt-ICP complexes recovered peroxidase-like activity, which promoted DAP production, accompanied by a pronounced fluorescence intensity ratio (I575/I465). In optimum conditions, the detection limits (LODs) were 2.79 ng/mL (colorimetric) and 0.26 ng/mL (ratiometric fluorescence), respectively. The feasibility of the proposed method was verified via three spiked samples, yielding recovery rates of between 86.08 % and 112.64 %, indicating the significant potential for the highly sensitive, accurate on-site detection of ICP in actual samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Isocarbophos (ICP) is a broad-spectrum organophosphate insecticide that effectively controls mites and Homoptera pests [1]. Compared with other organophosphorus pesticides (OPs), ICP is not only effective in pest prevention but also displays significant ovicidal properties [2], leading to its widespread utilization in the agricultural field. However, ICP residues pose a significant environmental risk, which can cause strong neurotoxicity in the human body via food chain bioaccumulation [3]. Therefore, the European Commission regulations and the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency stipulate maximum ICP residue levels (MRLs) of 0.01 mg/kg and 1 mg/kg, respectively, in agricultural products [2]. To reduce ICP toxicity to human health, the Ministry of Rural Agriculture of China has banned the sale and use of ICP since September 2024. The traditional methods commonly used for ICP detection and analysis include gas chromatography (GC) [4], gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS) [5], high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [6,7], and liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) [8]. Although these techniques are highly sensitive and accurate, they exhibited notable drawbacks, including the necessity for expensive instruments, specialized researchers, and complicated sample pretreatment procedures, rendering them unsuitable for on-site ICP detection.

Colorimetric methods show significant potential for on-site detection of pesticide due to simple operation, as well as rapid analysis and visualization. However, the practical application of colorimetric techniques is restricted by distinct drawbacks such as low sensitivity and limited resistance to interference. In recent years, the utilization of additional biosensors for the development of dual-mode detection methods has attracted considerable attention since these techniques yield two independent results that can mutually verify each other, consequently improving the accuracy of the detection outcomes. Deng et al. [9] developed a chemiluminescent-colorimetric method for detecting glyphosate by using it to regulate Fe3O4@Cu peroxide-like activity. Furthermore, single-atom iron nanozyme were used to develop a dual-mode electrochemical and colorimetric biosensor for organophosphorus pesticides detection in vegetables [10]. Among the various types of available biosensors, ratiometric fluorescence depends on the individual proportions of two fluorescence signals, which can be adjusted to reduce external interference and increase highly sensitive detection [11]. Therefore, combining the colorimetric method with ratiometric fluorescence to develop a dual-mode technique is the ideal strategy to enhance the sensitivity, visual clarity, and accuracy of the detection results. Liu et al. [12] utilized titanium carbide quantum dots to develop a colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence method for detecting curcumin and hypochlorite (ClO−). A novel colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence sensing method based on blue carbon dots (bCDs) was developed for the detection of glyphosate [13]. However, minimal studies are available regarding the utilization of the colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence method for ICP detection.

Recently, nanozymes have attracted significant attention for analysis and diagnostics. Nanozymes are nanomaterials that display enzyme-like properties, including peroxidase-like, oxidase-like and catalase-like. Compared with natural enzymes, nanozymes present advantages, including cost efficiency, excellent stability, and tunable catalytic properties. Therefore, various nanozymes have been used as signal-reporting elements to explore their potential applications in analysis and diagnostics. Since Fe3O4 nanozymes exhibit both peroxidase-like activity and superparamagnetism, they are commonly used in biosensors that integrate sample pretreatment and signal detection. However, single-component Fe3O4 nanozymes demonstrate low peroxidase-like activity, resulting in low detection sensitivity. Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are porous materials produced via the covalent bonding between metal clusters or ions and linkers, characterized by high porosity, outstanding mechanical stability and adjustable surface properties [14]. Therefore, MOFs are ideal support materials for loading nanozymes, facilitating the construction of composite nanozymes with elevated peroxidase-like activity, consequently enabling highly sensitive detection. Studies have shown that utilizing Fe3O4@MIL-100(Fe) nanozymes to detect glutathione displayed an limit of detection (LOD) of 0.26 μM [15], while a sensitive electrochemical sensor using Fe3O4@ZIF-4 identified bisphenol A with an LOD of 0.015 μM [16]. Among the various available MOFs, copper benzene-1,3,5-tricarboxylate (Cu-BTC) has drawn particular attention due to its cost efficiency and simple synthesis. Therefore, synthesis of Fe3O4@Cu-BTC would be great potential for construction of simple, high-sensitive detection method. Preliminary experiments have revealed that sulfur and nitrogen co-doped carbon nanodots (S, N-CDs) inhibit the peroxidase-like activity of Fe3O4@Cu-BTC, providing a novel approach for constructing highly sensitive dual-mode colorimetric-ratiometric fluorescence methods.

This study thoroughly examines the inhibitory mechanism of S, N-CDs on the Fe3O4@Cu-BTC composite nanozymes catalytic activity. The results show that S, N-CDs reduce ·OH generation and scavenge the subsequent ·OH. A novel colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence method is developed for ICP detection using S, N-CDs to inhibit catalytic Fe3O4@Cu-BTC nanozyme activity. As shown in Scheme 1 , aptamer (Apt)-modified Fe3O4@Cu-BTC nanozymes are hybridized with complementary chain (CS)-altered S, N-CDs to obtain Fe3O4@Cu-BTC/Apt-CS/S, N-CDs complexes. S, N-CDs significantly inhibit catalytic Fe3O4@Cu-BTC nanozyme activity, resulting in minimal to no peroxidase-like activity of Fe3O4@Cu-BTC/Apt-CS/S, N-CDs complexes, preventing hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) from oxidizing OPD to produce 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP). Here, the color of the solution appears faint yellow or colorless. In addition, at an excitation wavelength of 390 nm, a weak or absent fluorescence peak at 575 nm is noted for DAP, while S, N-CDs display a strong fluorescence peak at 465 nm. Contrarily, the presence of ICP causes CS/S, N-CDs dissociation from the Fe3O4@Cu-BTC/Apt. After magnetic separation, the Fe3O4@Cu-BTC/Apt-ICP complexes exhibit significant peroxidase-like activity, which promotes the formation of the yellow DAP product. Furthermore, a strong fluorescence peak of DAP appears at 575 nm, while the fluorescence peak at 465 nm disappears due to the absence of S, N-CDs. Ultimately, the dual-mode detection method is successfully established, offering several advantages, including high sensitivity, accurate, reliable results, and excellent visualization capacity, revealing significant potential for detecting ICP in agricultural products.

The 1,3,5-benzenetricarboxylic acid (H3BTC) and thiourea were obtained from Maclean Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China), while the O-phenylenediamine (OPD), l-arginine, bovine serum albumin (BSA), and glycine were supplied by Aladdin Reagents Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The glacial acetic acid (HAc), sodium acetate (NaAc), copper sulfate pentahydrate (CuSO4 ·5H2O), ferric chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O), H2O2 (30 %) and anhydrous ethanol were purchased from Chengdu Kelong Chemical Co. Ltd. (Chengdu, China). The ICP was supplied by the National Pesticide Quality Inspection Center (Beijing, China). All reagents used in this study were analytically pure.

The ICP Apt sequence and complementary strand (CS) were 5′–NH2–AGCTTGCTGCAGCGATTCTTGATCGCCACAGAGCT-3′ and 5′–NH2–GAATCGCTGCAGCAA-3′, respectively, which were obtained from the previous study [17] and synthesized by Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The underline sections of the ICP aptamer corresponded to CS.

The morphology and size of the nanozymes were measured via scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (Merlin Compact, Zeiss, Germany), while the chemical composition of nanozymes was analyzed via Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (PerkinElmer Life and Analytical Sciences, Boston, MA, USA). X-ray diffraction (XRD) was used to assess the crystal structure of the nanozymes (Bruker AXS D8 Advance, Bruker AXS GmbH, Karlsruhe, Germany). The fluorescence signal was measured using an RF-6000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Instrument (Suzhou) Co. Ltd, China) while the nanozyme activity was determined by a SpectraMax i3x (Molecular Devices, Inc., Silicon Valley, CA).

Before Fe3O4@Cu-BTC nanozyme synthesis, Fe3O4 was prepared using a previously described solvothermal method [18]. The detailed steps are provided in the Supporting Information. The Fe3O4@Cu-BTC nanozymes were prepared according to a previous method delineated by Yao et al. [19], with slight modifications. Here, 100 mg Fe3O4 dispersed in 20 mL anhydrous ethanol was sonicated for 30 min, followed by the addition of 50 mL of a 100 mM CuSO4·5H2O solution (dissolved in anhydrous ethanol). The mixture was reacted at 70 °C for 60 min while shaking at 220 rpm. Next, 50 mL of a 100 mM H3BTC was added to the above mixture and stirred for at 70 °C for 4 h. The reaction products were magnetically separated and washed with a 50 % (v/v) ethanol aqueous solution three times. The obtained Fe3O4@Cu-BTC nanozymes were dried overnight in a 50 °C vacuum oven and kept at 4 °C for subsequent experiments.

The S, N-CDs were synthesized using a previous method described by Lei et al. [20] with slight modifications. First, 1.37 g thiourea and 1.15 g citric acid were added to 20 mL ultrapure water, followed by stirring to obtain a uniform and transparent solution. After being heated at 180 °C for 5 h, the products were passed through a 0.22 μm membrane, followed by dialyzing for 12 h and freeze-drying to obtain the S, N-CDs. Finally, the S, N-CDs were stored at 4 °C in the dark for subsequent use.

First, 10 mg of the Fe3O4@Cu-BTC nanozymes was dispersed in 4 mL of a crosslinking agent containing 30 mM NHS and 20 mM EDC and mixed thoroughly. The dispersion was reacted for 30 min at room temperature to activate the carboxyl group on the Fe3O4@Cu-BTC nanozyme surfaces. Then, 100 μL NH2-Apt (4 μM) was added and then incubated at 37 °C for 2 h, after which the subsequent Fe3O4@Cu-BTC/Apt was washed three times with PBS. This was followed by resuspension in PBS and storage at 4 °C for subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the CS/S, N-CDs probe
ce:section.ce:para: First, 60 mg S, N-CDs were evenly dispersed in 6 mL PBS buffer (10 mM, pH = 7), after which 50 mg NHS and 50 mg EDC were added and mixed thoroughly. The mixtures were oscillated for 30 min at room temperature to activate the carboxyl group on the surface of S, N-CDs. Then, 100 μL of NH2-CS (40 μM) was added and incubated for 24 h, after which the S, N-CDs/CS was stored at 4 °C for subsequent use.

First, 50 μL Fe3O4@Cu-BTC/Apt (2.5 mg/mL) and 100 μL CS/S, N-CDs (150 μg/mL) were added to an EP tube, followed by the addition of 200 μL STE buffer and incubation at 37 °C for 40 min to obtain the Fe3O4@Cu-BTC/Apt-CS/S, N-CDs complexes. Excess CS/S, N-CDs were removed via magnetic separation, after which the complexes were resuspended in 350 μL of PBS. Next, 50 μL of an ICP solution with different concentrations was added and incubated at 200 rpm at 37 °C for 30 min. After washing three times with PBS via magnetic separation, 550 μL of HAc-NaAc buffer (pH = 6.0, 55 mM), 100 μL of an OPD solution (150 mM), and 100 μL of an H2O2 solution (375 mM) were added to the precipitation and reacted at 25 °C for 10 min. A SpectraMax i3x and a fluorescence spectrophotometer were employed to determine the absorbance at 450 nm and the fluorescence intensity at 465 nm and 575 nm, respectively. In addition, the Color Picker app (v3.1.0) on a smartphone was used to convert the color of the solution into RGB values for on-site analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of ICP in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Brassica chinensis, beans, and apples were purchased from a local supermarket and used for actual sample analysis. The samples were pretreated using a previously described method [21]. The Brassica chinensis, beans, and apples were washed three times with water to remove surface contaminants and placed in a fume hood to dry overnight. Then, 1 g of the sample was weighed and placed in a bottle, after which 5 mL of ultrapure water was added, followed by sonication for 2 min, and then being left to stand for 5 min. Next, different concentrations of ICP standard solutions were added and mixed thoroughly. Finally, the supernatant was collected for ICP analysis using the proposed dual-mode detection method.

The Fe3O4 nanozymes were nearly spherical in shape and had an average diameter of around 200 nm (Fig. 1 A). Cu-BTC introduction caused the agglomeration of many small particles of the Fe3O4@Cu-BTC nanozymes surfaces (Fig. 1B), which was consistent with previous studies [22,23]. The Fe3O4 and Fe3O4@Cu-BTC nanozymes exhibited zeta potential values of −24.1 mV and −27.2 mV, respectively (Fig. 1C). This was ascribed to a large number of H3BTC carboxyl groups on Fe3O4 nanozyme surfaces. The Fe3O4 nanozyme curves presented 2θ peaks at 18.3°, 30.00°, 35.32°, 42.90°, 53.40°, 56.72°, and 62.28°, which corresponded to the (111), (220), (311), (400), (422), (511), and (440) crystal planes, respectively (Fig. 1D) [24], indicating successful Fe3O4 nanozyme synthesis. In addition, the Fe3O4@Cu-BTC nanozymes exhibited two new diffraction peaks at 12° and 74.2°, corresponding to the (222) and (220) crystal planes of cubic Cu-BTC, respectively [19,21]. The characteristic peaks FTIR of the Fe3O4 nanozymes at 590 cm−1, 1386 cm−1, 1633 cm−1 and 3430 cm−1 were ascribed to the stretching vibration peaks of the Fe–O, C–O, CO and O–H bonds, respectively (Fig. 1E) [25]. Furthermore, an additional peak at approximately 436 cm−1 were mainly assigned to the stretching vibration of the Cu–O bond [26]. The two absorption peaks of Fe3O4@Cu-BTC nanozymes at 1633 cm−1 and 1451 cm−1 were attributed to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching of CO in the carboxyl group, respectively [27]. These results indicated successful Fe3O4@Cu-BTC nanozyme synthesis. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to examine the composition and chemical states of the Fe3O4@Cu-BTC elements. Complete XPS spectral analysis showed that the synthesized nanozymes contained C, O, Fe, and Cu elements (Fig. S1A). The peaks located around 288.7eV, 286.2 eV and 284.8 eV in the C1s spectrum corresponded to the CO, C–O and C–C bonds of BTC (Fig. S1B). The two peaks at 533.1 eV and 531.7 eV in the O1s spectrum were assigned to the C–O and CO bonds, respectively (Fig. S1C), indicating the presence of the BTC [28]. The peak at 530.3 eV was attributed to the metal-O bond. As shown in Fig. S1D, the peaks at 711 eV and 724.2 eV were ascribed to Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2 of Fe (II), while those at 713.8 eV and 726.8 eV corresponded to Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2 of Fe (III), respectively. This indicated the presence of Fe2+ and Fe3+ on the Fe3O4@Cu-BTC surface. As depicted in Fig. S1E, the peaks at 933 eV and 952.9 eV were associated with Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2 of Cu (I), respectively, while those at 935.1 eV and 955.1 eV corresponded to Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2 of Cu (II), respectively. This indicated the presence of Cu2+ and Cu + on the Fe3O4@Cu-BTC surface.

The peroxidase-like activity of the Fe3O4@Cu-BTC nanozymes was nearly 1.4-fold higher than in Fe3O4 (Fig. 1F). This may be because the Fe3O4@Cu-BTC nanozymes present a larger specific surface area, which increases the likelihood of contact between the active site and the catalytic substrate, consequently enhancing the Fe3O4@Cu-BTC nanozyme peroxidase activity. In addition, the Fe3O4 magnetic saturation (Ms) was 68.08 emu g−1 (Fig. S2). Although the Ms of Fe3O4@Cu-BTC nanozyme was reduced to 45.32 emu g−1 after introduction of Cu-BTC, they can be rapidly collected in 15 s via an external magnetic field (inset of Fig. S2). These results indicated that Fe3O4@Cu-BTC nanozymes could be used for sample pretreatment and signal detection.

The S, N-CDs displayed uniformly dispersed, granular, small particles (Fig. 2 A). The FTIR spectra of S, N-CDs at approximately 669 cm−1, 1401 cm−1, 1637 cm−1, and 3449 cm−1 corresponded to the C–O–C, C–N, CO, and O–H stretching vibration, respectively (Fig. 2B) [20,29]. These results indicated successful S, N-CDs synthesis. A slight redshift appeared in the maximum emission wavelength of S, N-CDs when the excitation wavelength increased from 370 nm to 410 nm (Fig. 2C). At an excitation wavelength of 390 nm, the S, N-CDs exhibited the strongest emission peak at 465 nm, which was consistent with previous studies [20]. These results indicated successful S, N-CDs synthesis.

The zeta potential of the Fe3O4@Cu-BTC nanozymes and Apt were −27.15 mV and −11.05 mV, respectively, while that of the Apt/Fe3O4@Cu-BTC probe was −23.67 mV (Fig. S3A). This may be because the Apt amino group interacted with the Fe3O4@Cu-BTC nanozyme carboxyl group, which changed the surface charge of the Fe3O4@Cu-BTC nanozymes [30]. In addition, after coupling with Apt, the Apt/Fe3O4@Cu-BTC probe displayed a typical nucleic acid absorption peak at 260 nm (Fig. S3B). These results indicated successful Apt/Fe3O4@Cu-BTC synthesis.

The zeta potential of the CS and S, N-CDs were −9.39 mV and −9.99 mV, respectively, while that of the CS/S, N-CDs probe was −6.17 mV (Fig. S4A). In addition, the CS/S, N-CDs probe demonstrated a typical DNA absorption peak at 260 nm (Fig. S4B). These results indicated successful CS/S, N-CDs synthesis.

As shown in Fig. 3 A and B, the presence of only HAc-NaAc/H2O2/OPD in the system resulted in low absorbance intensity at 450 nm and a weak fluorescence signal at 575 nm, indicating minimal OPD oxidation to DAP. Adding Fe3O4@Cu-BTC nanozymes to the system significantly enhanced the absorbance value at 450 nm and the fluorescence intensity at 575 nm, indicating that the Fe3O4@Cu-BTC nanozymes displayed strong peroxidase-like activity, resulting in OPD oxidation into DAP to produce fluorescent and colorimetric signals. Introducing S, N-CDs into the system substantially decreased the absorbance value at 450 nm and the fluorescence signal at 575 nm, indicating that the presence of S, N-CDs inhibited the peroxidase-like activity of the Fe3O4@Cu-BTC nanozymes.

Then, the feasibility of dual-mode detection was verified using two probes and different ICP solution concentrations. In the absence of ICP, only a single strong fluorescence peak was evident at 465 nm at an excitation wavelength of 390 nm (Fig. 3C). Contrarily, the presence of ICP significantly decreased the fluorescence peaks at 465 nm, while a new fluorescence peak appeared at 575 nm. At a higher ICP concentration, the fluorescence peaks at 465 nm disappeared, and the fluorescence peak at 575 nm increased significantly. Therefore, the ICP fluorescence assay was conducted utilizing the I575/I465 fluorescence intensity ratio. The absence of ICP significantly reduced the absorbance peak at 450 nm (Fig. 3D), while the solution displayed a faint yellow color (inset of Fig. 3D). Increasing the ICP concentration significantly elevated the absorbance value at 450 nm and intensified the solution color. This indicated that the ICP colorimetric assay could be performed by measuring the absorbance value at 450 nm or via naked-eye observation. Therefore, these results verified the feasibility of the colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence method for ICP detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism of Fe3O4@Cu-BTC catalytic activity inhibition by S, N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Since the suppression of Fe3O4@Cu-BTC activity by S, N-CDs was crucial for developing the colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence detection method, the inhibitory mechanism was investigated thoroughly. The kinetic enzyme parameters were examined to evaluate the effect of CS/S, N-CDs on the affinity between Fe3O4@ Cu-BTC/Apt and the substrates. The detailed procedures are provided in the Supporting Information. Fig. S5 illustrates the typical enzymatic activity curves according to the Michaelis-Menten model for the Fe3O4@Cu-BTC, Fe3O4@Cu-BTC/Apt, and Fe3O4@Cu-BTC/Apt-CS/S, N-CDs complexes. K m value of nanozymes is an important kinetic parameter to describe its affinity with substrates. The lower the K m value indicates the higher the affinity of nanozyme to substrates. Apt addition changed the K
m of the Fe3O4@Cu-BTC nanozymes from 0.14 mM to 0.18 mM, when OPD was used as a substrate (Table 1 ). When H2O2 was used as a substrate, the K
m of the Fe3O4@Cu-BTC nanozymes decreased from 0.59 mM to 0.36 mM. These indicated that Apt slightly increased the affinity of Fe3O4@Cu-BTC toward H2O2 [31]. The K
m values of the Fe3O4@Cu-BTC/Apt-CS/S, N-CDs complexes were 3.44- and 1.56-fold higher than that of Fe3O4@Cu-BTC/Apt when using OPD and H2O2 as substrates, respectively, indicating the S, N-CDs decreased the affinity of Fe3O4@Cu-BTC/Apt toward the two substrates.

Three kinds of hydrogen donor substrates (OPD, ABTS and TMB) were employed to investigate the correlation between the inhibitory level and substrate type. The inhibitory effect was initiated by the respective addition of the three substrates (Figs. 4 A, B, and C). Furthermore, a higher S, N-CDs concentration enhanced the degree of inhibition, indicating that the hydrogen donor substrate type did not influence the suppressive effect.

Subsequently, the effect of S, N-CDs on the H2O2 substrate was investigated. Electron spin resonance (EPR) tests displayed that the Fe3O4@Cu-BTC nanozymes mainly produced the ·OH and O2 ·- reactive oxygen species (ROS) (Fig. 4D). In addition, free radical scavenging tests were employed to investigate the generated ROS, using p-benzoquinone (PBQ), d-histidine, and isopropanol (IPA) reagents, which were capable of scavenging O2 •–, 1O2, and ·OH, respectively. Compared with the H2O2 group, only IPA significantly decreased the absorbance (Fig. 4E), suggesting that the Fe3O4@Cu-BTC nanozymes mainly generated ·OH free radical. This differed from the EPR tests, likely due to the varied environmental conditions in these two tests. The O2 •– free radical was more unstable in the aqueous solution than in the methyl alcohol used during the EPR tests. Therefore, the Fe3O4@Cu-BTC nanozymes primarily produced ·OH in the aqueous solution. Changes in ·OH content before and after S, N-CDs addition to the system were examined using a terephthalic acid (TA) capture experiment described in a previous study [9] and the detail information was shown in the supporting information. When only Fe3O4@Cu-BTC nanozymes were present, a strong fluorescence peak in the system was evident at 430 nm, indicating the generation of a large amount of ·OH (Fig. 4F). Adding S, N-CDs decreased the fluorescence peak intensity of the system, indicating a decline in the ·OH content. The ·OH content was reduced in two ways. One involved blocking the active catalytic site of the Fe3O4@Cu-BTC nanozymes with S, N-CDs, which hampered the catalytic reaction between Fe3O4@Cu-BTC and H2O2, as evidenced by the enzyme kinetics experiment. The other involved ·OH scavenging by S, N-CDs, the occurrence of which was verified via a TA capture experiment in the S, N-CDs/H2O2 system. Absorption peaks were evident at 430 nm when only H2O2 was present in the system, indicating that the H2O2 produced ·OH (Fig. 4G). Adding a small amount of S, N-CDs to the system decreased the fluorescence peak intensity at 430 nm, confirming the ·OH scavenging ability of S, N-CDs. A similar phenomenon was observed by Chong et al. [32], who showed that graphene quantum dots (GQDs) efficiently scavenged several free radicals, including ·OH, O2 •–, and DPPH·.

Therefore, a possible mechanism for the inhibition of the Fe3O4@Cu-BTC nanozyme activity by S, N-CDs can be ascribed to the following: (1) The presence of S, N-CDs hampered the reaction between Fe3O4@Cu-BTC and H2O2, which reduced the ·OH produced by the Fe3O4@Cu-BTC nanozymes. (2) The S, N-CDs scavenged the generated ·OH (Scheme 1).

To further improve the performance of the dual-mode method, some key parameters were optimized, including the amount of substance of Apt (A), the Apt-to-CS ratio (B), the amount of S, N-CDs (C), the hybridization time (D), the H2O2 concentration (E), concentration the OPD concentration (F), the pH (G), and HAc-NaAc buffer concentration (H). As shown in Fig. S6, at higher H2O2, OPD, and HAc-NaAc concentrations, the colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence signals displayed an initial increase, followed by stabilization. An increase in the hybridization time, amount of substance of Apt and amount of S, N-CDs initially decreased the colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence signals, followed by stabilization. The colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence signals exhibited an initial decline at a lower Apt-to-CS ratio, followed by an increase. The colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence signals reached the minimum value at the ratio of Apt to CS of 1:2. In theory, the aptamer was hybridized with the CS at the ratio of 1:1. In fact, both the surface of Fe3O4@Cu-BTC nanozyme and the surface of S, N-CDs have many coupling sites. Therefore, there were many aptamers and CS on the surface of Fe3O4@Cu-BTC nanozyme and S, N-CDs, respectively, which resulted in the presence of steric hindrance when aptamer was hybridized with complementary strand. Therefore, more S, N-CDs were required to provide more CS to hybridize with aptamer on the surface of Fe3O4@Cu-BTC nanozyme, which can overcome the steric hindrance The similar results were observed by the previous studies [33,34]. At a higher pH, the colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence signals showed an initial increase, followed by a decline. Therefore, the optimal detection conditions consisted of the amount of substance of Apt 5 pM, an Apt-to-CS mole ratio of 1:2, the amount of S, N-CDs of 15 ng, a hybridization time of 30 min, an H2O2 concentration of 50 mM, an OPD concentration of 20 mM, pH 6, and an HAc-NaAc buffer solution concentration of 40 mM.

To investigate the sensitivity of the colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence method, ICP concentrations from 1.5 ng/mL to 173 ng/mL were used to obtain the corresponding colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence signals. Higher ICP concentrations gradually increased the absorbance value at 450 nm (Fig. 5 A). The absorbance value displayed an excellent linear relationship with ICP concentration in a range of 24.18–60.76 ng/mL (Fig. 5B). The linear fitting equation y = 0.0139x-0.0973 (R2 = 0.9959) while the LOD was 2.79 ng/mL based on 3σ/S [9]. An RGB model was also used to record the detection signal, yielding a linear fitting equation y = 0.0159x+0.0643 (R2 = 0.9401) and a LOD of 9.43 ng/mL (Fig. 5C).

Higher ICP concentrations gradually increased the fluorescence intensity at 575 nm, while decreasing the fluorescence intensity at 465 nm (Fig. 5D). The fluorescence intensity ratio (I575/I465) presented a good linear relationship with ICP concentrations ranging from 30.45 to 76.49 ng/mL (Fig. 5E), yielding a linear fitting equation of y = 0.2766x-7.8603 (R2 = 0.9658) and a LOD of 0.26 ng/mL. Compared with other available detection methods, the colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence method in this study exhibited lower LODs (Table S1). In addition, the dual-mode detection method produced two independent results for mutual verification, increasing the accuracy of the results.

To verify the specificity, five common pesticides (aldicarb, phoxim, omethoate, carbofuran, and carbaryl) and five potential matrix interferences (BSA, trypsin, glycine, l-arginine, and citric acid), were selected and compared with the detection result of ICP. As shown in Figs. S7A and B, only ICP demonstrated high absorbance and ratiometric fluorescence signals, highlighting the high selectivity of the dual-mode method for ICP detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of ICP in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: Three samples including Brassica chinensis, beans, and kumquats, were used to evaluate the feasibility of the proposed method. The colorimetric method yielded ICP recovery rates of 102.56%–112.64 %, with relative standard deviations of 0.23%–2.47 % (Table S2). The ICP recovery rates for the ratiometric fluorescence method ranged from 86.08% to 94.41 %, with relative standard deviations ranging between 0.67 % and 4.14 %. These results indicated the dual-mode method had the excellent stability and accuracy, proving the suitability for detecting ICP in real samples.

In this study, we successfully synthesized Fe3O4@Cu-BTC nanozymes with high peroxidase-like activity, the peroxidase-like activity of which was first discovered to be inhibited by S, N-CDs. The inhibitory mechanism can be ascribed to the strong ability of S, N-CDs to decrease and scavenge the ·OH produced by the Fe3O4@Cu-BTC nanozymes. Based on this principle, a novel colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence dual-mode detection method was developed for ICP detection, using S, N-CDs to inhibit the catalytic Fe3O4@Cu-BTC nanozyme activity. Compared with other biosensors, the dual-mode biosensor displayed high sensitivity (0.26 ng/mL), excellent result visualization capability, and simple procedures without complex pretreatment. In addition, two independent results (colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence results) can verify mutually, increasing the accuracy of the detection results. Considering these advantages, the dual-mode detection method demonstrates significant potential for the highly sensitive, accurate, on-site detection of ICP in real samples.

Lijun Wang: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Investigation, Conceptualization. Jiangchuan Tang: Writing – original draft, Data curation. Shanshan Ren: Writing – review & editing. Haixia Hu: Investigation, Data curation. Xiao Yang: Supervision. Xianggui Chen: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Lijun Wang has patent pending to China National Intellectural Property Administration. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was supported by Science and Technology Department of Sichuan Province (2024NSFSC1244 and 2022YFN0016) and National Key Research and Development Program of China (2023YFF1104800).
